lens.included_activities: Civilian federal, state, and local courts of law and sheriffs' offices conducting court functions, assessed operationally; under the fixed acquisition lens there are no qualifying independent private LMM firms because the industry is public administration.
lens.excluded_activities: Military courts, American Indian and Alaska Native tribal courts, private law firms, prosecutors and public defenders, police, corrections, probation and parole, private arbitration, court technology vendors, contractors, shells, captive internal units, non-control financing vehicles, and all entities outside the roughly $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Courts provide constitutionally and statutorily mandated adjudication, case administration, records, hearings, orders, and public access, funded principally through government appropriations and partly through prescribed fees rather than recurring outsourced commercial contracts or market pricing.
lens.deviation_from_default: none; applying the fixed default lens produces no qualifying private-firm population because NAICS 922110 is a public-administration activity

a.bridge: BLS and O*NET describe judge, hearing-officer, and court-clerk tasks across government occupations rather than a wage-weighted task census for NAICS 922110. I map document intake, completeness checks, docketing, scheduling, record maintenance, transcription support, search, summarization, legal research, draft preparation, public information, fee administration, and routine correspondence to exposure, while discounting adjudication, hearings, credibility assessment, jury management, emergency orders, security-sensitive work, and final legal decisions. National occupation data can overstate court exposure because court-municipal-license clerks include noncourt workers and understate it where judicial law clerks and specialized case staff are important.
a.rationale: Court operations contain large document, data, research, scheduling, and communication workloads suited to AI assistance or avoidable hiring. The legally consequential core requires human judgment, procedural fairness, and accountable decisions, leaving the central exposed share below one-half of wage-weighted work.
a.change_evidence: A court-system time-and-wage study linking judges, judicial law clerks, court clerks, reporters, administrators, interpreters, IT, and support staff to detailed tasks and current automation status would materially change the range.
caveats: No source provides a six-digit court-industry wage-weighted task distribution.
Task and staffing mixes vary sharply by jurisdiction, case type, court level, and use of elected clerks or shared administrative offices.

rho.rationale: Document processing, summarization, internal search, transcription support, guided forms, scheduling, public-information tools, and anomaly flagging can be implemented within five years. Realization is constrained by due process, explainability, hallucination and bias risk, confidentiality, sealed records, cybersecurity, procurement, legacy case-management systems, accessibility, records retention, union and civil-service rules, fragmented state and local governance, and the requirement that humans make consequential decisions.
rho.change_evidence: Audited five-year deployment cohorts across state and local courts reporting task-level adoption, staff hours, error and appeal rates, privacy incidents, procurement duration, integration cost, user access, and human-review burden would materially narrow the range.
caveats: NCSC documents current experiments and use cases, not realized labor substitution across courts.
Low-risk ancillary administration can move faster than legal research, fact interpretation, or decisions affecting individual rights.

e.rationale: The official NAICS definition places civilian courts in Sector 92 Public Administration. Government courts do not satisfy the fixed population of independent LMM firms supplying outsourced services to external customers, so the eligible share is structurally zero even though frozen n is missing and must not be replaced.
e.change_evidence: Only a classification change or authoritative establishment-level evidence of privately owned firms properly classified in NAICS 922110, rather than vendors classified elsewhere, would change the structural zero.
caveats: Frozen n is missing; the zero is an eligibility share based on the industry's public nature, not an estimate of establishment count.
Private court-service and technology contractors may be acquisition candidates in other NAICS codes but are outside this record.

s5.rationale: With no eligible independent firms, there is no qualifying private control transfer. Elections, judicial appointments, reorganizations, shared-services arrangements, procurement awards, and changes in government administration do not transfer control of an eligible operating company.
s5.change_evidence: Authoritative evidence that a privately controlled court operator is properly classified in NAICS 922110 and can undergo a conventional control transaction would change the estimate.
caveats: This is a structural transaction definition, not an assessment of turnover among judges or court administrators.
Outsourcing a court function to a vendor is a procurement event, not a control transfer of the court.

q.rationale: No defensible commercial-retention quantity exists because courts do not sell the service basket through market-priced recurring contracts and have no receipts denominator. Efficiency gains may become capacity, shorter queues, service quality, lower appropriations, or reallocated budgets, none of which maps consistently to the operator-retained gross-benefit construct.
q.change_evidence: A court-specific public-value framework with audited benefit accounting and jurisdiction-level appropriation rules showing how technology savings are retained, reallocated, or returned would be needed to define and estimate an analogue.
caveats: Prescribed filing fees are not a market price for adjudication and do not create a conventional gross-margin retention mechanism.
Assigning a neutral value would falsely imply commercial evidence.

d5.bridge: NCSC measures aggregate state-court filings and the Administrative Office reports federal filings, while BLS projects employment in selected judicial occupations; none directly forecasts five-year constant-quality demand for the full NAICS 922110 service basket. I use 70 million state filings in 2024, up 4% from 2023, broad federal filing increases in fiscal 2025, and projected 2024-34 growth of 3% for court, municipal, and license clerks and 1% for judges and hearing officers to anchor modest growth. Filing counts can overstate demand when case complexity falls or digital self-service reduces work and understate it when complexity, hearings, language access, security, or backlog work rises.
d5.rationale: Population, commerce, family and housing disputes, crime, bankruptcy, and statutory rights sustain court demand. Recent state and federal filings rose, but long-run state filings remain below 2012, diversion and alternative resolution can reduce cases, and workload is volatile by case type, supporting modest central growth with a broad range.
d5.change_evidence: A jurisdiction-weighted five-year forecast combining filings, pending caseload, case weights, hearings, trials, self-represented litigants, digital transactions, population, legislation, and funded judicial capacity would materially change the ratio.
caveats: The 70 million state-filing figure aggregates unlike case types and reports filings rather than weighted workload.
Federal caseload is a small, volatile share of total US court activity and was affected by multidistrict litigation and bankruptcy cycles.

o.rationale: Virtually all year-five adjudication and legally binding court administration still require an accountable public court operator. Software can enable self-service, screen documents, schedule matters, and assist research, but it cannot independently exercise sovereign judicial power, ensure due process, conduct hearings, protect rights, issue enforceable judgments, or bear institutional accountability.
o.change_evidence: Enacted law and validated deployments permitting software to issue final binding judgments without human judicial accountability across material case volumes would change this estimate.
caveats: The estimate concerns institutional operator necessity, not employee counts or the amount of human work per case.
Online dispute resolution and guided self-service may eliminate parts of the current basket but not the accountable court institution.

narrative.executive_view: Courts have meaningful assistive AI potential in document, research, docket, scheduling, records, and public-service workflows, but risk and governance sharply limit five-year implementation in rights-affecting decisions. Demand is durable and requires an accountable judiciary. The code is structurally outside the acquisition lens: it is public administration, frozen n is missing, the eligible-firm and transfer shares are zero, and commercial retention cannot be defined from appropriations and prescribed fees.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can assist intake, completeness checks, docketing, scheduling, transcription, summarization, record search, legal research, draft preparation, guided forms, public information, and anomaly flagging. NCSC reports current court use in document retrieval, chatbots, and guardianship-report screening. Privacy, bias, hallucination, due process, cybersecurity, accessibility, and human-review requirements make ancillary administration more implementable than fact interpretation or final decisions.
narrative.value_capture: There is no conventional commercial value-capture mechanism. Courts are funded through appropriations and prescribed fees, so productivity may appear as shorter queues, avoided hiring, more reviewed cases, service quality, budget reallocation, or lower future appropriations. Without a public-value accounting framework and jurisdiction-specific budget rules, translating those outcomes into retained gross benefit would be artificial.
narrative.firm_availability: The official industry consists of civilian courts of law within Public Administration. It contains no qualifying independent LMM firms under the fixed lens, and frozen n is missing. Judicial appointments, elections, procurements, reorganizations, and outsourcing contracts do not constitute a qualifying control transfer of the court; private technology and support vendors belong to other industries.
narrative.demand_durability: State courts reported 70 million filings in 2024, up 4% from 2023 but still 27% below 2012, while several federal filing categories increased in fiscal 2025. Population and legally enforceable rights sustain demand, although diversion, alternative dispute resolution, policy changes, digital self-service, and case-mix shifts alter workload. BLS expects only modest growth in judges and court-related clerks over the next decade.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The decisive risks are structural ineligibility and undefined commercial retention. Operational uncertainties include privacy and sealed records, biased or fabricated output, appeal and due-process risk, procurement delays, legacy systems, cybersecurity, fragmented governance, accessibility, labor rules, public trust, and extreme variation in case complexity. Aggregate filing counts are weak proxies for weighted workload.
narrative.principal_driver: Large document and administrative workloads create real AI-assisted capacity gains while court demand and accountable judicial authority remain durable.
narrative.principal_weakness: NAICS 922110 is public administration with no eligible private-firm population or conventional commercial-retention construct.

sources.title: 2022 North American Industry Classification System Manual: 922110 Courts
sources.supports: Official scope in Sector 92 Public Administration: civilian courts of law and sheriffs' offices conducting court functions, excluding military and tribal courts.

sources.title: O*NET Job Duties: Court, Municipal, and License Clerks
sources.supports: Task proxy covering court-document filing, administrative work, record maintenance, document verification, minutes, and docket or calendar preparation.

sources.title: Judges and Hearing Officers
sources.supports: Judicial task and operator evidence: judges research and evaluate legal information, conduct hearings, apply law, issue decisions, and work for federal, state, or local government; employment is projected to grow 1% from 2024 to 2034.

sources.title: Information Clerks
sources.supports: Court-clerk task and demand proxy: court clerks maintain records, prepare dockets, and receive and send documents; court, municipal, and license clerk employment is projected to rise from 180,400 in 2024 to 185,900 in 2034.

sources.title: NCSC Trends in State Courts 2025
sources.supports: Current AI evidence: courts use AI for document retrieval, docketing and scheduling, chatbots, summaries, and guardianship-report screening, while privacy and rights-affecting decisions require strong controls and human final decisions.

sources.title: National Center for State Courts: Data for Court Professionals
sources.supports: National state-court demand proxy reporting 70 million filings in 2024, up 4% from 2023 and down 27% from 2012.

sources.title: Judicial Business 2025
sources.supports: Federal demand proxy: fiscal 2025 regional appeals rose 5% to 41,824, district filings rose 6% to 382,692, and bankruptcy filings rose 11% to 557,376.
